observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to: 
a) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer and confirm outstanding payables aged two or more years and require the concerned suppliers, officers and employees to submit necessary documents to support their claims; 
b) revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund accordingly the long outstanding payables and for which no actual claim has been filed, or not covered by perfected contract upon approval of the Sangguniang Bayan; and 
c) evaluate and effect the necessary correcting/adjustment entry to reflect the correct amounts of Accounts Payable, Due to Officers and Employees, Due to NGAs, Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable, and Other Payables.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant stated that payables outstanding for ten years or more without claimants will be considered for reversion to unappropriated balance to be used to fund other much needed appropriations of the Municipality. She committed to draw adjusting and correcting entries to reflect the correct amounts. The Municipal Treasurer committed to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the unclaimed checks and will inform the payees via written notifications and be given a reasonable period to respond.
observations.reason: Long outstanding payables still noted reverted to the unappropriated surplus. Proof of reversion of the unappropriated surplus has yet to be submitted to the Audit Team; necessary correcting/adjustment entry has yet to be effected.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management: 
a) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to avoid certifying existence of available appropriation and allowing payment for procurement of costumes and uniform for the use of Municipal employees or officials in excess of the allowable amount; 
b) strictly adhere with the provision of COA Circular 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012 on the prohibition against excessive expenditures and Administrative Order No. 6, s. 2017 on the continued adoption of austerity measures of the government; and 
c) require the Municipal employees or officials to stop the practice of procurement of costumes and uniform for the use of Municipal employees or officials in excess of the allowable amount.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant observed the deadlines set in Sections 70, 72, 73 of MNGAS, Volume I, and Section 41(2) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, and Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001. The Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant avoid certifying existence of available appropriation and allowing payment for procurement of costumes and uniform for the use of Municipal employees or officials in excess of the allowable amount.
observations.reason: Municipality still procure uniforms in excess of allowable amounts; No action taken to strictly adhere with COA Circular 2012-003 and Administrative Order No. 6, s. 2017.

observations.recommendation: b) oblige the Municipal Treasurer to intensify tax collection efficiency by continually implementing the measures carried out by her office in order to minimize the reported delinquencies.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer devises collection strategy to increase tax collection.
observations.reason: Collection of taxes is still low; the Municipal Treasurer still devises collection strategy to increase tax collection.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a) require the Municipal Agriculture Officer to: (i) comply with the statutory period prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-386 relative to the filing of request for relief of accountability; (ii) report birth of offspring, distribution, deaths and transfer of ownership of breeding stocks to the Municipal Accounting Office; b) direct the Municipal Accountant to record the necessary adjustments for the birth, distribution, deaths and changes in fair market value of their Biological Assets and thereafter prudently apply all of the accounting rules and regulations prescribed under IPSAS 27 and maintain the Work, Other Animals and Breeding Stocks Ledger Cards; c) require the Municipal Agriculture Office and Municipal Accountant to: (i) make representation with the Department of Agriculture and Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE) to verify whether these breeding stocks are still carried in their books or transferred to the Municipality; and (ii) regularly reconcile their records for the acquisitions, births, distribution, deaths and disposal of Biological Assets in order to determine the proper valuation and to confirm the existence of such.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Agriculture Office were in the process of gathering data as to births, deaths and transfer of breeding stocks. The required report of birth of offspring, distribution, deaths and transfer of ownership of breeding stocks necessary in the preparation of adjustment was not submitted to the Accounting Office. Reconciliation of records by the Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal Agriculture Office has yet to be conducted.
observations.reason: Management has yet to take action regarding the recommendation; No updates have been provided to date; Reconciliation was not conducted; Required reports and adjustments were not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to endeavor to enforce civil remedies for collection of taxes and the related surcharges and interests resulting from delinquency to oblige/encourage defaulting taxpayers to pay their obligations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer continuously collecting delinquent taxes and encourages defaulting taxpayers to pay their obligations.
observations.reason: Civil remedies were not enforced for the collection of delinquent taxes; Collection remains low.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management: a) create an Appraisal Committee to compute and determine the proper value of the removed/replaced parts/materials that need to be derecognized in the books or use the cost of the replacement; and b) require the Municipal Accountant to derecognize in the books, based on the report of the Appraisal Committee or the cost of the replacement, the costs of replaced/removed materials incident to the improvements, rehabilitations and renovations/repairs made.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant implemented derecognition where applicable; Appraisal Committee has yet to be created.
observations.reason: Appraisal Committee has yet to be created; Derecognition in the books for the subject replacement was still not effected.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant require the concerned contractors to refund the excess in contract cost of ₱2,483,782.98.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer sent letters to concerned contractors on June 26, 2025 informing these contractors on the findings during inspection by the COA Technical Audit Group and demand immediate rectification of the noted deficiencies. Management has yet to take any action regarding the recommendation.
observations.reason: Excess in contract cost of ₱2,483,782.98 were still not refunded; No updates have been provided to date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to derecognize from its books those unserviceable properties properly disposed.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant implemented derecognition where applicable.
observations.reason: Unserviceable properties were still not disposed during the year.

observations.recommendation: The Audit Team recommended that the Management require the attendees to refund part of the registration fee equivalent to one day.
observations.managementAction: No action taken; attendees still did not refund part of the registration fee.
observations.reason: Attendees still did not refund part of the registration fee.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the Management: a) take the necessary actions to expedite the collection of the unsettled disallowances; b) subsequently, cause all the liable certifying and approving officers, who took part in the disbursement of disallowed expenditures, to assume responsibility in the final settlement of the remaining balance of the disallowance; and c) ensure that before allowing settlement of disallowances through installment payment the same was authorized and approved by COA.
observations.managementAction: Only ₱22,500.00 or 2.38% of the recorded total audit disallowances of ₱945,160.32 were settled during the year. The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant continue to send demand letters and undertake collection efforts; liable certifying and approving officers did not assume responsibility in the final settlement of the disallowance; no approval from COA on the settlement of disallowance through installment.
observations.reason: Collection of disallowed amounts is low; Liable certifying and approving officers still did not assume responsibility; No COA approval on installment settlements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a) enforce the COA Order of Execution on the Notice of Disallowances
which became final and executory to settle the outstanding balances;
b) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer i) reconcile their
records with the SASDC and effect adjustment if any; and ii) to take the
necessary actions to expedite the collection of the unsettled
disallowances;
c) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) exhaust all possible remedies to
collect from each of the liable persons; and (ii) subsequently, cause all the
liable certifying and approving officers, who took part in the
disbursement of disallowed expenditures, to assume responsibility in the
final settlement of the remaining balance of the disallowance; and
d) direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that any request for
settlement of disallowances through installment payment is duly
authorized and approved by COA prior to implementation.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Municipal Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to conduct
reconciliation of their records to correct the reported balances of
Inventories at year-end, any discrepancies between the RPCI and the
Accounting Records should be investigated and adjusted accordingly;
and
b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting
entries to recognize as expense the issued semi-expendable properties, in
accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2024-006.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant to:
a) re-compute the depreciation of affected PPEs by observing the correct
starting point of recognizing depreciation;
b) once re-computation is completed, prepare the appropriate journal entry
to adjust the depreciation in order to reflect the correct amount of its i)
Accumulated Depreciation; ii) Property Plant and Equipment; iii)
Depreciation Expense; and
c) provide residual value for infrastructure related PPEs, except for road
networks, in accordance with Section 6.1 (f) of COA Circular No. 2015-
002.

We recommended that the Management:
a) require the Accounting Office and MAO to update their respective
property schedules with complete and consistent descriptions to facilitate
tracing, identification, and reconciliation of recorded properties; and
b) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary entries and
record the cost of the land upon the completion of the land titling

We reiterated our recommended the Management to require the:
a) Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs
owned by the Municipality and submit the RPCPPE not later than the
prescribed date of submission; and
b) Municipal Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to reconcile
their records to correct the reported balances of PPEs at year-end, any
discrepancies between the RPCPPE and the Accounting Records should
be investigated and adjusted accordingly.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management:
a) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the ensuing budget
preparation the appropriation of sufficient amount for the insurance
coverage for all insurable properties of the Municipality;
b) require the General Services Officer to facilitate the gathering of all the
necessary documents needed and submit the same to the GSIS as basis
for the assessment of general insurance coverage; and
c) ensure that all properties to be acquired by the Municipality in the future
are properly insured with the GSIS in compliance with RA No. 656.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer:
a) ensure that all taxes withheld are filed and remitted, complete with
required attachments, in accordance with the manner and timing provided
in RA 8424, to avoid tax deficiencies that may warrant further BIR tax
audit/investigation;
b) make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the Due to BIR account for
the taxes withheld and transferred from the SEF, TF, and PhilHealth Fund
to the GF; and
c) ensure the timely and accurate transfer of withheld taxes from the SEF,
TF, and PhilHealth Fund to the GF to maintain updated and accurate
records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Municipal Treasurer to remit in full all premium contributions and loan
payments of employees to the GSIS on or before the 10th day of the month
following the month which the contributions apply, in accordance with
the schedule provided in Section 5 of RA 8291; and
b) Municipal Accountant to conduct analysis and periodic reconciliation
with the GSIS to avoid unreconciled balances and interest charges, and
the Municipal Treasurer to settle any resulting unpaid contributions
immediately.

We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to:
a) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to verify the validity and
existence of outstanding payables aged two years or more, and require
the concerned suppliers, officers, and employees to submit the necessary
documents to support their claims;
b) revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund accordingly
the long outstanding payables and for which no actual claim has been
filed, or not covered by perfected contract upon approval of the
Sangguniang Bayan; and
c) ensure that only valid transactions supported with complete
documentations are recorded as payables to avoid erroneous or
unsupported obligations of the Municipality.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Management require
the Municipal Accountant to:
a) coordinate with the Provincial Accounting Office to address/resolve any
discrepancies between the reciprocal accounts, Due from LGUs and Due
to LGUs; and
b) effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct amounts of
receivable, payable and income accounts in the books at year-end.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Municipal Engineer (i) require the concerned contractors to rectify the
noted defects by the TAS on the contracted works; (ii) ensure timely
implementation of infrastructure projects by monitoring the
performance of the contractors and implement the calibrated measures
mentioned in GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 in response to the delays in the
implementation of infrastructure projects; (iii) instruct the preparer of
the ABC to show details in the cost analysis accompanied with plans and
specifications, continuously update the market prices of all construction
inputs and use only unit prices that are realistic based on valid up-to-date
information; and (iv) cause the immediate submission of the lacking
Approved PERT/CPM or Bar Chart/S-Curve to the Audit Team, for
further review;
b) Municipal Inspectorate Committee check compliance with the
recommended corrective actions;
c) in case the contractors did not satisfactory rectify the noted defects,
require them to refund the amount equivalent to the cost of deficiencies
of ₱1,632,008.86 otherwise, institute appropriate legal action against the
erring contractors; and
d) Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant (i) impose and collect
the correct amount of liquidated damages to the concerned contractors
amounting ₱230,883.42; and (ii) require the concerned contractors to
refund the excess in contract cost of ₱216,101.39.

We recommended the Management to:
a) strictly adhere to the guidelines in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002 for cash advance in general and in COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 for Confidential and Intelligence Fund; and
b) ensure that all cash advances shall be fully liquidated at the end of each year to avoid the case of unreported expenses and overstatement of the corresponding receivables and equity accounts, and understatement of the related expenses which affects the fairness of the reported amounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality adhered to the guidelines in the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management require the: 
a) Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO)/MTO in the absence of GSO, to properly include every type of inventory in the RPCI and record the amounts at the lower of cost and net realizable value; and
b) Municipal Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to conduct reconciliation of their records to correct the reported balances of Inventories at year-end, any discrepancies between the RPCI and the Accounting Records should be investigated and adjusted accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO)/MTO in the absence of GSO, submitted RPCI semi-annually.
observations.reason: The MAO and Inventory Committee conducted partial reconciliation of their records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to require the: 
a) Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs owned by the Municipality and submit the RPCPPE not later than the prescribed date of submission; 
b) Municipal Accountant to rectify the incorrect reclassification of the various items previously recognized as Other Machineries and Equipment and establish accounts for Semi-Expendable Property, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004 and supplementary guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2024-006 for the updated list of new accounts for Semi-Expendable Property; and
c) Municipal Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to reconcile their records to correct the reported balances of PPEs at year-end, any discrepancies between the RPCPPE and the Accounting Records should be investigated and adjusted accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant effected reclassification of item previously recognized as Other Machineries and Equipment to Semi-expendable property accounts.
observations.reason: There were still discrepancies noted totaling ₱230,919,099.02 in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management to require the Municipal Accountant to: 
a) re-compute the depreciation of affected PPEs by observing the correct starting point of recognizing depreciation; and
b) once re-computation is completed, prepare the appropriate journal entry to adjust the depreciation in order to reflect the correct amount of its i) Accumulated Depreciation; ii) Property Plant and Equipment; iii) Other Supplies Inventory; iv) Total Assets; and v) Government Equity; vi) Depreciation Expense; and vii) Income.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant recompute the provisions of depreciation of affected PPEs. The Municipal Accountant prepared appropriate journal entries for the affected accounts under JEV Nos. 24124451 and 24124452 and JEV No. 2412331 dated December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management to: 
a) designate a focal person or create a Committee which will be responsible for the registration of all real estate owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386; and
b) upon completion of the land titling, require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary entries and record the cost incurred in the purchase or fair value if acquired through donation or transfers without cost of the land; c) ensure that the Municipality has absolute or legal ownership over the properties before implementing the construction of buildings and other structures and covered by Transfer Certificate of Titles.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor was designated as the focal person responsible for the registration of all real estate owned by the Municipality. The Municipality through the Municipal Assessor were able to register 14 parcels of land.
observations.reason: 44 parcels of land remained not registered under the Torrens Title System as of December 31, 2024; Municipal Accountant has yet to prepare adjusting entries.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management: 
a) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the ensuing budget preparation the appropriation of sufficient amount for the insurance coverage for all insurable properties of the Municipality; 
b) require the General Services Officer to facilitate the gathering of all the necessary documents needed and submit the same to the GSIS as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage; and
c) ensure that all properties to be acquired by the Municipality in the future are properly insured with the GSIS in compliance with RA No. 656.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer provided appropriation for insurance coverage of insurable properties of the Municipality. The MGSO continuously facilitating the gathering of the necessary documents needed by the GSIS. Additional two properties insured in CY 2024.
observations.reason: Sufficient appropriation for insurance coverage of all properties were still not provided; Necessary documents needed by GSIS was still not completely gathered.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to: 
a) coordinate with the Provincial Accounting Office to address/resolve any discrepancies between the reciprocal accounts, Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs; 
b) review guidelines/procedures to ensure consistency/uniformity in recording methods and confirm said procedure with the Provincial Accounting Office; and 
c) effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct amounts of receivable, payable and income accounts in the books at year-end.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant is in constant communication with the Provincial Accounting Office to address discrepancies in the reciprocal accounts. The Municipal Accountant continuously review their current procedures and devises steps/methods to ensure consistency in the recording with the Provincial Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Unreconciled amounts as of year-end were still noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office: 
a) make the necessary adjustment in the books to present the correct balance of the Due to BIR account in the financial statements; and 
b) submit the required alphalist of payees for taxes remitted during CY 2023 and ensure that all tax remittances be supported with documents required in BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23- 2014 and Revenue Regulations No. 2-2006 necessary for verification of the transactions.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer submitted the required alphalist of payees on June 2024. The Municipal Accountant explained that the noted difference was due to certain account which was recoded interchangeably with the Due to BIR account during the preparations of journals in May and October 2024; and the withholding taxes included in the liquidation of cash advances of two employees were still on process at the time of remittance. As a control measure, pre-remittance reconciliation and post-preparation rechecking were conducted.
observations.reason: Necessary adjustment in the books has yet to be submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the the Municipal Accountant draw the following necessary adjustment in order to present fairly the balances of the affected payable accounts in the financial statements: Due to Other NGAs – 363,801.06; Trust Liabilities – 363,801.06.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted JEV No. 24061867 dated June 26, 2024 for the adjustment of the affected accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management require the: 
a) Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to adopt strategies to fully utilize the allotted budget for the 20% Development Fund; 
b) MPDC, in coordination with Municipal Accounting Office to assess the unutilized balance whether there is a need for reprogramming/ realignment; and 
c) MPDC and Municipal Engineer to ascertain viability of their implementation and develop a corrective action plan to address the unimplemented and unfinished PPAs, prioritizing the completion of these projects within targeted timelines and ensuring proper budgetary allocation and utilization.
observations.managementAction: MPDC is in constant coordination with the Municipal Accounting Office in the monitoring of utilization of the 20% Development Fund for possible reprograming or realignment of unutilized amounts. MPDC is continuously monitor the implementation of PPAs in the LDRRM Plan.
observations.reason: The utilized amount for its current appropriations is ₱26,946,900.60 or 82.61% of its total current appropriation of ₱32,819,486.00 (for CY 2024 the reported utilized amount for its current appropriations is ₱26,946,900.60 or 82.61%).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management direct the LDRRMO to: 
a) continuously monitor and evaluate the implementation of PPAs in the LDRRM Plan; and 
b) coordinate with the concerned implementing offices/ department identified in the LDRRMFP to undertake steps in order to optimally utilize the DRRM fund such as monitoring the implementation of the PPAs to ensure that they are implemented within the target dates of completion and ensuring that the approved budget for the PPAs is sufficient for its implementation, among others, in order to fully or substantially implement the PPAs prioritized and included in their LDRRM Plan in support of disaster risk reduction and management activities towards a more disaster resilient community.
observations.managementAction: LDRRMO continuously monitor the implementation of PPAs in the LDRRM Plan.
observations.reason: The utilized amount of LDRRMF in CY 2024 is ₱11,027,938.77 or 65.75% out of its total appropriation of ₱16,773,397.05.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to direct the a) Local Finance Committee to analyze the nature of expenditures that exceeds the budgeted amounts, investigate reasons of incurrence and develop actions to prevent incurrence of deficit in the future; and b) Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to exercise diligence, prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality incurred deficit for CY 2024 amounting to ₱3,789,863.30 as reflected in the Statement of Financial Performance as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to: a) submit immediately the Pre-closing Trial Balance and Subsidiary Schedule of General Ledger at every twentieth after the end of the month; and b) be mindful of the deadlines and adhere strictly with the provisions of Sections 70, 72, 73 of MNGAS, Volume I, and Section 41(2) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, and Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 on the submission of trial balance, financial reports and year-end financial statements and supporting schedules of LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted Pre-closing Trial Balance and Subsidiary Schedule of General Ledger with the prescribed date. The Municipal Accountant observed the deadlines set in Sections 70, 72, 73 of MNGAS, Volume I, and Section 41(2) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, and Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe/follow updated description of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant observe the updated description of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following measures: a) Municipal Engineer require the concerned contractors to rectify the noted defects by the COA-TAS; b) after the corrective works, Municipal Inspectorate Committee check compliance with the recommended corrective actions; and c) in case of failure of the contractors to satisfactory rectify the noted defects, require them to refund the amount equivalent to the cost of deficiencies of ₱2,403,800.34 otherwise, institute appropriate legal action against the erring contractors.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer sent letters to concerned contractors to rectify the defects noted by COA-TAS.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management that:
a) 	the Municipal Accountant (a) recognize in the books the reconciling items identified in the Bank Reconciliation Statement in accordance with Item 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of PD No. 1445; and (b) make the necessary adjusting entries for the stale checks by debiting the Cash in Bank – LCCA account and crediting Accounts Payable to reflect the correct balances of the said accounts; and
b) 	the Municipal Treasurer (a) issue written notices to payees of the existence of the checks at least one month before a check become stale; and (b) submit the List of Unreleased Checks as of year-end to the MAO as their basis for the preparation of Journal Voucher to record the entry for the restoration of cash in bank and recognition of the corresponding accounts payable for the unreleased/unclaimed checks and reversal of said entry in the ensuing year.

direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) trace and record the adjustments in order to reconcile the differences between the recorded amount of RPT and SET receivables and the records of Real Property Tax Delinquencies maintained by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and the corresponding deferred income account; and (ii) establish the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card; and
require the Municipal Treasurer to promptly submit to the Municipal Accounting Office a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to: (i) adopt stringent measures and effective strategies in the collection of RPT and SET Receivables to improve collection efficiency; and (ii) endeavor to enforce civil remedies for collection of taxes and the related surcharges and interests resulting from delinquency to oblige/encourage defaulting taxpayers to pay their obligations.

direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments to record the cost of unissued accountable forms, stamps, business plates and stickers to the Accountable Forms, Plates and Stickers account; and
require the General Services Officer to: (i) prepare the Report of Physical Count of Inventories on all inventory accounts reported at year-end; and (ii) coordinate with other offices like MTO, OCR and Permits and Licenses Section for the preparation of RPCI of accountable forms in their possession at year-end.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to submit a schedule of insurance paid for individual vehicles indicating the expired and unexpired portion of the insurance and consequently effect adjustment on the total insurance expense recorded in CY 2021 to recognize as asset the unexpired portion pursuant to the accrual method of accounting.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Management:
a. 	require the Municipal Agriculture Officer to (i)  comply with the statutory period prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-386 relative to the filing of request for relief of accountability; (ii) report birth of offspring, distribution, deaths and transfer of ownership of breeding stocks to the Municipal Accounting Office; and (iii) investigate the sale of cattle by one of the beneficiaries of the Cattle Feedlot Fattening Program and take the necessary action against him for violating the terms and conditions set in the Animal Dispersal Contract;
b. 	direct the Municipal Accountant to book up acquisition of breeding stocks and the necessary adjustments for the birth, distribution, deaths and changes in fair market value of their Biological Assets and thereafter prudently apply all of the accounting rules and regulations prescribed under IPSAS 27 and maintain the Work, Other Animals and Breeding Stocks Ledger Cards; and
c. 	require the Municipal Agriculture Office and Municipal Accountant to (i) make representation with the Department of Agriculture and Department of Labor and Employment to verify whether these breeding stocks are still in their books or transferred to the Municipality; and (ii) regularly reconcile their records for the acquisitions, births, distribution, deaths and disposal of Biological Assets in order to determine the proper valuation and to confirm the existence of such.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Management:
direct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) strictly observe/follow updated description of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009; (ii) reclassify the expenses to proper inventory, PPE or expense account; and (iii) furnish copy of the Revised Chart of Accounts to all offices/end-users for proper identification of accounts in the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) to be submitted to the Municipal Budget Office; and
b. 	instruct the ICO-Municipal Budget Officer to adopt the new account codes as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the preparation of the agency’s budget.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that computations for the payment of compensation-related magna carta benefits are in accordance with the guidelines provided in Republic Act No. 9433 and its IRR, DBM-DSWD Joint Circular No. 1, s.2018 and DBM-DOH JC No. 1-2012; and
adhere strictly to the provisions provided for in the payment of hazard pay, subsistence allowance and laundry allowance to avoid possible disallowance in audit.

In view of the above, we recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Budget Officer to observe Sections 322, 328 and 350 of RA No. 7160 in the use of appropriations and refrain from charging prior year expenses against the current legislative appropriation for lack of legal basis.

Foregoing considered, we recommended the following measures:
Municipal Engineer (i) submit a satisfactory explanation on the observations and findings of the COA-TAS in the Technical Inspection Reports transmitted to their office; and (ii) require the concerned contractors to rectify the noted defects by the COA-TAS;
after the corrective works, Municipal Inspectorate Committee check compliance with the recommended corrective actions; and
in case of failure of the contractors to satisfactory rectify the noted defects, require them to refund the amount equivalent to the cost of deficiencies of ₱2,403,800.34 otherwise, institute appropriate legal action against the erring contractors.

we recommended that the Management (a) direct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to demand the payment of the possible liquidated damages totalling ₱230,883.42 from the concerned contractors; and (b) direct the Municipal Engineer to: (i) ensure timely implementation of infrastructure projects by monitoring the performance of the contractors and implement the calibrated measures mentioned in GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 in response to the delays in the implementation of infrastructure projects; and (ii) submit the Revised PERT/CPM and/or Bar Chart/S-Curve showing the projected and actual physical accomplishments as of date specified in the individual TIR.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Engineer instruct the preparer of the ABC that all assumptions in generating the estimates be shown in details in the cost analysis accompanied with plans and specifications, continuously update the information/statistics on market prices of all construction inputs and use only unit prices that are realistic based on valid up-to-date information; and
Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant require the concerned contractors to refund the excess in contract cost of ₱2,483,782.98.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to examine thoroughly all disbursement vouchers as to necessity, validity and completeness of the supporting documents to avoid excessive and unnecessary claims and payment of expenditures without proper and complete documentations.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended the Management to:
require the LDRRMO to review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to incorporate the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF from previous years as required under Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002;
ensure the full implementation of the PPAs as designed in the LDRRMFIP; and
require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended/unobligated balances of the 30% QRF and the 70% DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at year-end as prescribed in Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management refrain from using the 20% Development Fund for expenses not prescribed under the pertinent laws, rules and regulations relating to the defined usage or utilization of the fund.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the Management to:
a. 	require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustment to transfer the LGSF-DRRAP from the General Fund to the Trust Fund in accordance with Section 3.7 of LBC No. 134;
b. 	expedite the implementation of the PPAs to benefit the intended beneficiaries and meet the purpose for which the fund was created; and
c. 	submit pictures as proof of posting of the reports to three conspicuous places and the copy of the notices sent to various government stakeholders bearing the date of receipt of the said notice.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management:
direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) trace and record the adjustments in order to reconcile the differences between the recorded amount of RPT and SET receivables and the records of Real Property Delinquencies maintained by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and (ii) to establish the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card; and
require the Municipal Treasurer to promptly submit to the Municipal Accounting Office a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year.

In view of the above, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the noted difference in the reciprocal accounts and effect necessary adjustments to reflect the correct account balance as of year-end in order to achieve fair presentation of the said accounts in the financial statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the Management to:
require the GSO to record the unserviceable properties separately to IIRUP and to start the disposal process of the unserviceable properties following the proper procedures for the disposal of assets; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to: i) record depreciation of PPE monthly; ii) to record the impairment loss of the unserviceable assets still with book value either by completely impairing the remaining carrying values or record impairment loss to the extent of the assessed salvage value of such properties; and iii) derecognize from its books those unserviceable properties properly disposed with the required supporting documents i.e. IIRUP.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the Management to (a) create a Committee which will be responsible for the registration of all real estate owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386; (b) upon completion of the land titling, require the Municipal Accountant to; i) prepare the necessary entries and record the cost incurred in the purchase or fair value if acquired through donation or transfers without cost of the land; and ii) record the lands with market value of ₱859,410.00 in its books after obtaining certified copy of the documents used in the titling of the said lands and ensuring that these are not identified or the same with any of the lands recorded in the books; and (c) direct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that the Municipality has absolute or legal ownership over the properties before implementing the construction of buildings and other structures.

In this regard, we recommended the Management to immediately require all accountable officers to (a) maintain and update individual cashbooks daily and to serve as reference in reconciling the records of the accountable officer and the Municipal Accountant; (b) issue official receipts in a strictly sequential manner; and (c) closely monitor the accuracy and physical existence of cash on hand by reconciling daily the cash on hand with the cash balance in the cashbook pursuant to Section 63 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue enforcing the settlement in favor of the government. Persons responsible and liable who are still employed in the Municipality should be required to immediately settle the outstanding audit disallowances. Furthermore, employ all possible means provided by law for the settlement of disallowances for those who are already separated from government service, otherwise those other persons determined to be liable shall settle the same.

We recommended and the Management agreed to (i) instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the perpetual inventory method in the recording of inventory and issuance of supplies and materials as provided under the IPSAS 12 and (ii) the Supply/Property Officer to prepare the necessary RIS and SSMI to support the recording of expenses in the books of accounts and submit to the Municipal Accountant to adjust the concerned inventory account.
Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant stated that the MGT will need additional manpower in the Municipal General Services Office and a stockroom for the inventory items to effectively implement the audit recommendations. In response, the Municipal Mayor expressed their willingness to undertake the said measures to effectively carry out the recommendations.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) adopt the principles prescribed in IPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the Municipality’s PPE and for the fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements; and (b) coordinate with the MGSO to record the unserviceable properties in the IIRUP which she will need in the disposal of the subject unserviceable properties.

We recommended and the Management agreed to refrain from paying travelling expenses/allowances and overtime pay to individuals hired through job order contract relative to their claims in connection with the discharge of tasks and or attendance to conference/training/seminar since this practice is an indication of an employer-employee relationship, or may be considered as such, that circumvents the above cited provisions.
Management Comment:
The Municipal Accountant explained in her letter dated April 1, 2019 that the individuals hired through job order are no longer allowed to claim travelling expenses and per diem in compliance with the audit recommendations contained in the Audit Observation Memorandum.
The Municipal Accountant further clarified that the payment for the services during Saturdays, Sundays and holidays of the individuals hired through job order who were assigned in the Accounting and Treasury Office were erroneously accounted and treated as overtime pay instead of regular pay/wages. Those personnel were hired to provide necessary services during December 1-31 and January 1-31 of calendar year 2018. They were required and expected to render services on Saturdays, Sundays and holidays to complement the existing workforce to meet the deadline of the submission of year-end reports. She however insisted that the CSC rules were not violated because what was given to the individuals hired through job order was the rate on a daily basis and not the amount based on the standard computation for overtime pay and the Municipality did not consider their services as government service.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team stressed that the hiring of individuals through job order should be supported by Activity Designs that clearly indicate the duration or period and job to be undertaken. Furthermore, the Management should strictly adhere with the provisions of CSC Resolution No. 020790 dated June 5, 2002 and CSC-DBM JC No. 2015-2 dated November 25, 2015 relative to allowances and travelling expenses granted to individuals hired through job order contracts.

We recommended and the Management agreed to refrain from granting monthly telephone allowance to those who already have monthly subscription from local mobile service providers to prevent excessive expenditure.
Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the Management justified that the grant of telephone allowances was not excessive since the cellphone load given only amounted to ₱900.00.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team reminded the Management that prudence should be observed in any government transactions. Moreover, it was stressed that Management should review the grant of mobile phone to employees since Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 23, 2012 includes “provision of mobile phone, whether postpaid line subscription or prepaid, in excess of one unit for each entitled official which should not be lower than Division Chief rank” as a case that is considered as an excessive expenditure of government funds.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Engineer and all officers designated as focal units, to strictly comply with the policy guidelines and procedures in the implementation of LGSF-ADM and LGSF-AM Programs enumerated in DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-3 dated May 23, 2017 and DILG MC No. 2018-61 dated May 3, 2018.
Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the Municipal Engineer commented that the projects were already 100% complete and signified his intention to comply with the policies and procedures in their future undertakings especially in the implementation of LGSF funded projects.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to strictly observe the timeline on the submission of POs to facilitate timely rendition of reports by the Auditor. Moreover, prepare the POs and its supporting documents in complete detail in order to safeguard the interest of the government in all its executed contracts.
Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the Management commented that the distance between the Municipal Government Office and Commission on Audit (COA) hinders their compliance with the prompt submission of POs within five working days from issuance thereof. They added that it is time consuming and uneconomical on their part to travel frequently to COA’s office only to submit PO.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team stands firm on its recommendations. The Management should observe the provisions on submission of POs together with the supporting documents. Moreover, it is emphasized that POs and its supporting documents should be prepared in complete detail in order to safeguard the interest of the government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Local Chief Executive require the Accounting Unit and Supply Property Unit or the Municipal Treasurer to investigate the discrepancy between the PPE accounts recorded in the books with that appearing in the RPCPPE to effect reconciliation between the two (2) records. In order to achieve this, there is a need to update regularly the PPE Ledger Cards of the accounting unit as well as property cards of the property unit/Municipal Treasurer, pursuant to Section 114 , Volume I of NGAS for LGUs.

Stop charging prior-year’s obligation against Current Legislative  Appropriation of the ensuing year for lack of legal basis;
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to observe Section 328 of RA 7160 in the obligation of funds and include cost of unpaid electricity incurred during the period of December as among the accounts payables of the municipality at year-end.

In this regard, we recommended the Management to seek the refund of the respective amounts paid to the former Municipal Elective Officials that represent the cash equivalent of the five-day mandatory leave that were not deducted from their respective vacation leave credits.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant be required to prepare computation of savings for CY 2014 CNA Incentive supported with proper documentation such as approved re-alignment of fund to facilitate validation of declared savings and determination of correct amount of CNA Incentive to be granted to employees and management. Direct them to exclude savings from Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses considering it was not among the source of fund for CNA Incentive.  Likewise, instruct them also to submit the following:
Certification from Human Resource Management/Administrative Officer as to total number of rank-and-file employees in the agency; and
Certificate of accreditation issued in favor of the accredited employees’ organization.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated that the participating councilors be required to submit basis for payment of excessive registration fees and inclusion of tours of delegates during the PCL Quarterly National Board Meeting, Island Congress and other activities. Likewise, direct the Vice-Mayor to select only a representative from SB members who shall attend the meeting and re-echo new knowledge learned during the undertaking, thereby inspiring them the prudent use of government fund.

We recommended that the municipality adhere strictly with Section 15 of RA 9344 which provides that one percent (1%) of  the internal revenue allotment shall be set aside as an appropriation for the strengthening and implementation of the programs, projects, and activities of the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children. Moreover, undertake steps to fully implement the vital programs/projects prioritized and included in the annual investment program and as mandated by the Act for the protection of children.  These projects’ implementations are meant to provide children of the municipality with those activities that are enumerated under the foregoing DILG Memorandum Circular that is anchored with RA 9344. In this end, they would also be dealt with appropriate measures advantageous to their welfare and well-being.

In view of the above, we recommended the submission of copies of contracts together with the supporting documents, within five days from the perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001, for prompt evaluation by the Auditor and to point out to management the defects of the orders/contracts and the project itself, should there be any, and to finally effect timely corrective measures and to immediately submit the pertinent documents of the abovementioned projects to facilitate the conduct of post audit and preparation of the Inspection Report by the COA Technical Audit Specialist.

In view of the above, we recommended that the municipality fully implement projects funded out of the 20% development fund for the benefit of its constituents. The Municipal officials should strictly adhere to Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 of the DILG and DBM on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Community Development Fund to ensure efficient output, come up with projects with developmental impact that would improve the socio-economic condition of the greater number of its constituents.

We recommended that the municipality implement fully all the programs/projects/activities included in the LDRRMF to raise the level of its preparedness and save lives and properties of its constituents in times of emergencies, calamities and disasters. Furthermore, the management should give priority to the procurement of basic rescue and response equipment and conduct of municipal-wide trainings and refrain from using the 70% mitigation fund for purpose not directly related to the disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We reiterated the following recommendations: a. Require the Municipal General Services Office to reconcile the Property Cards with the Municipal Accountant’s Property Ledger Card to ensure fair presentation of the balance of PPE in the financial statements. Direct her to refer to previous years’ accounting records, inventory reports of the Barangays, Memorandum Receipt and/or Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment as reference to determine persons liable who received the Communication Equipment issued by the agency. Likewise, instruct her to coordinate with the Barangay Officials who may have the knowledge of receipt of Communication Equipment From the Municipal Government. Finally, instruct her to include in the RPCPPE all PPE owned by the Municipality including those under SEF and TF. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain PPE ledger card for each Property, Plant, and Equipment as required in the Manual of New Government Accounting System Vol. I. Moreover, compute and recognize Depreciation Expense on those PPE procured during the 1990s and make the necessary entry to record them in the Journal of Entry Voucher to avoid overstatement of book value of PPE and Government Equity accounts.

The Municipal Accountant had already maintained the property ledger for PPE and updated regularly.

Depreciation expenses were already recognized and corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers were already prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to submit their basis  for rating “Passed” the aforementioned legal, technical and financial documents submitted by the bidder despite above-mentioned deficiencies to avoid disallowance in audit. observations.recommendation: We recommended the following: a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Treasurer, to review thoroughly documents supporting payment of contract prior to payment. Supporting documents for the Post-Qualification process such as BAC Minutes and Resolution were submitted by TWG and BAC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following: a. Require the BAC to observe the provisions of IRR of R.A. 9184 in the posting of notice of award, notice to proceed and the approved contract. Moreover, require the BAC Secretariat to issue certification on the posting of the aforementioned documents in any conspicuous place in the premises of the procuring entity. b. Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to review thoroughly the documents supporting payment and stop the processing of payment to contractor if BAC would fail to post the aforementioned documents in the PhilGEPS website and website of the procuring entity, if any, and in any conspicuous place in the premises of the procuring entity designated for the purpose.

Documents were presented by the BAC Secretary during the Exit Conference.
In addition, the posting of such documents is a requirement in the Full Disclosure Policy of the DILG and one of the criteria in the Seal of Good Local Governance.

observations.recommendation: We recommend the following measures: a. Compel the accountable officers concerned to immediately liquidate their cash advances; otherwise, demand full payment of the amount advanced to them; b. Issue demand letters to the remaining accountable officers that were not issued with the same relative to their outstanding cash advances to comply with the provisions of the circular; and c. Inform them that failure on their part to comply herewith shall constitute a ground for the withholding of the payment of the money due them as provided for by COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and a “prima facie” presumption under Article 217 of the Revised Penal Code that they have malversed the funds to their personal interest and benefit (Martinez vs. Sandiganbayan, GR No. 126413 dated August 10, 1999). Their failure shall otherwise constitute violation of Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code for failure to render accounts and of Section 89 of PD 1445 which are punishable under Section 128 of the same code.
observations.managementAction: The total amount of                    P 27,120.00 was already settled by the accountable officers concerned in February 2016 according to the Municipal Accountant.
